{{Short description|Production of an electromotive force across a conductor in a changing magnetic field}}
{{Infobox physics
| name = Electromagnetic induction
| image = Solenoid-1.png
| caption = A changing [[magnetic field]] through a coil induces an [[electromotive force]].
| topics = [[Faraday's law of induction]], [[Magnetic field]], [[Electric field]], [[Maxwell's equations]], [[Lenz's law]]
}}

'''Electromagnetic induction''' (also known as '''Faraday's law of induction''') is the production of an [[electromotive force]] (EMF) across an electrical conductor in a changing [[magnetic field]]. It was discovered by [[Michael Faraday]] in 1831 and mathematically described by [[James Clerk Maxwell]] as [[Faraday's law of induction]], one of [[Maxwell's equations]].

== Faraday's law ==
{{Main|Faraday's law of induction}}
The induced EMF in a closed circuit equals the negative rate of change of [[magnetic flux]] <math>\Phi_B</math> through the circuit:

:<math>\mathcal{E} = -\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}</math>

where the magnetic flux is <math>\Phi_B = \int \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{A}</math>.

In differential form (Maxwell–Faraday equation):
:<math>\nabla \times \mathbf{E} = -\frac{\partial\mathbf{B}}{\partial t}</math>

== Lenz's law ==
{{Main|Lenz's law}}
The negative sign in Faraday's law encodes [[Lenz's law]]: the induced current flows in a direction such that its magnetic field opposes the change in flux that produced it. This is a consequence of [[conservation of energy]].

== Methods of induction ==
An EMF can be induced by:
# Changing the magnetic field strength (e.g., varying current in a nearby coil)
# Moving a conductor through a magnetic field
# Changing the area of a circuit loop in a magnetic field
# Changing the orientation of a coil relative to the field

== Motional EMF ==
For a conductor of length <math>L</math> moving with velocity <math>\mathbf{v}</math> through a uniform magnetic field <math>\mathbf{B}</math>:
:<math>\mathcal{E} = BLv</math> (when <math>\mathbf{v} \perp \mathbf{B}</math>)

== Applications ==
* [[Electrical generator]] — converts mechanical energy to electrical energy
* [[Transformer]] — changes AC voltage levels using mutual induction
* [[Induction motor]] — electric motor using induced currents
* [[Induction heating]] — heating metals via eddy currents
* [[Wireless charging]] — power transfer through inductive coupling
* [[Induction coil]] ([[Tesla coil]]) — high-voltage generation
* [[Metal detector]]s — detecting metal via induced eddy currents

== Eddy currents ==
Changing magnetic fields induce circulating currents ([[eddy current]]s) in conductors, causing energy loss (eddy current heating) and magnetic damping. Laminated transformer cores reduce these losses.

== See also ==
{{Portal|Physics}}
* [[Faraday's law of induction]]
* [[Lenz's law]]
* [[Magnetic field]]
* [[Maxwell's equations]]
* [[Transformer]]

== References ==
{{Reflist|30em}}

[[Category:Electromagnetic induction| ]]
[[Category:Electromagnetism]]
[[Category:Michael Faraday]]
